title: Moratorium & Commencement
content:
Session 5: Commencement of CIRP 
Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel India Limited [2019 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Following Arcelormittal order, on 18 October 2018, it informed the RP and the CoC that it had made the payments as 
directed.  
‚û¢‚Äã Numetal failed to make such payments, leading ArcelorMittal to resubmit its resolution plan dated 2 April 2018.  
‚û¢‚Äã On 19 October 2018, this plan was evaluated by the CoC and subsequently, ArcelorMittal was declared the 
successful resolution applicant. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The plan was then submitted to the NCLT, where OCs alleged discriminatory treatment vis-√Ý-vis FCs.  
‚û¢‚Äã HELD: On principles of equity, fair play, and the Wednesbury unreasonableness test, OCs‚Äô dues must be treated 
similarly to those of FCs ‚Üí directed FCs to sacrifice components of their recoveries, such as interest.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Dissatisfied, FCs appealed to the NCLAT. 
‚û¢‚Äã HELD: There can be no difference between financial and operational creditors in payment of dues under a 
resolution plan.  
‚û¢‚Äã All creditors ‚Äì financial and operational ‚Äì be paid 60.7% of their admitted claims and directed that profits 
generated during the CIRP be shared equally among all creditors.  
‚û¢‚Äã Further held that (i) the CoC could not constitute a sub-committee, (ii) Section 53 of the Code (liquidation 
waterfall) did not apply during CIRP, and (iii) certain claims affirmed by the adjudicating authorities were final. 
‚û¢‚Äã Also found that Standard Chartered Bank (SCB), an unsecured financial creditor with a claim of INR 3,462 crore 
(to whom NCLT had allocated only INR 60 crore), was unfairly discriminated against ‚Üí enhanced SCB‚Äôs payout 
to INR 2,160 crore, reasoning that the CoC, dominated by secured creditors such as SBI, had acted 
discriminatorily since SCB was unsecured and in the minority. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appeal to the SC 
‚ùñ‚Äã  
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the NCLAT was correct in holding that financial and operational creditors must be treated equally under a 
resolution plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the NCLT/NCLAT could review or alter the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom in approving a resolution plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether unequal treatment between secured and unsecured creditors violates the principle of equality under Article 14. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether delegation of CoC functions to a sub-committee was valid under the Code. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the statutory period of 330 days under Section 12, including time spent in judicial proceedings, was mandatory or 
directory. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 30(2) & (4) of the IBC: The RP must ensure that the resolution plan provides for payments to OCs not less than 
liquidation value, while the CoC may approve the plan by a 66% majority, based on feasibility and viability. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 31: The NCLT can approve a resolution plan if it meets Section 30(2) conditions; judicial review is limited to these 
parameters. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 53: Liquidation waterfall applies only during liquidation, not resolution. 
‚û¢‚Äã 53(1) ‚Äì list of priority 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 38 of CIRP Regulations (2016): Resolution plans must specify how OCs‚Äô interests are dealt with and ensure 
priority payment to OCs over FCs, but not necessarily equality in quantum. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 21 & 24: Only financial creditors constitute the CoC and vote on plans; OCs may attend but cannot vote. 
43 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 12 (as amended in 2019): The outer limit for CIRP completion is 330 days, including time taken in legal 
proceedings. 
‚ùñ‚Äã K. Sashidhar v. Indian Overseas Bank (2019) and Swiss Ribbons v. UOI (2019): Courts cannot substitute their 
judgment for the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom; equitable treatment applies only to similarly situated creditors. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RP‚Äôs role is administrative, not adjudicatory ‚Üí he collects and verifies claims, ensures completeness of plans, and facilitates 
CoC meetings.  
‚û¢‚Äã Decision-making rests entirely with the CoC, which exercises its commercial wisdom to determine the feasibility 
and viability of plans, including how funds are to be distributed among classes of creditors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Role of  prospective RA 
‚û¢‚Äã PRA has a right to receive complete information as to the CD, debts owed by it, and its activities as a going 
concern, prior to the admission of an application u/s 7, 9, 10 ‚Üí it has a right to receive the information contained 
in the information memorandum as well as the evaluation matrix mentioned in Regulation 36B 
‚û¢‚Äã THEN ‚Üí their plan must provide for measures as may be necessary for the insolvency resolution of the corporate 
debtor for maximisation of the value of its assets, which may include transfer or sale of assets or part thereof, 
whether subject to security interests or not. The plan may provide for either satisfaction or modification of any 
security interest of a secured creditor and may also provide for reduction in the amount payable to different classes 
of creditors ‚Äì see Regulation 37. 
‚û¢‚Äã Reg 38(1) ‚Üí must contain a provision that the amount due to OCs shall be given priority in payment over FCs + 
include provisions as to how to deal with the interests of all stakeholders including FCs and OCs [reg. 38(1A)].  
‚û¢‚Äã Must provide for the term of the plan, management and control of the business of the CD during such term, and 
its implementation.  
‚û¢‚Äã Must also demonstrate that it is feasible and viable and that the RA has the capability to implement the said plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FCs and OCs are inherently different under the Code.  
‚û¢‚Äã FCs provide capital, bear long-term risk, and are part of a regulated banking structure; OCs supply goods and 
services and have the option to halt supplies upon default.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, expecting equal recovery for both categories undermines the Code‚Äôs objectives and could lead 
financial creditors to prefer liquidation over resolution. 
‚û¢‚Äã Referring to Swiss Ribbons, the Court clarified that ‚Äúequitable treatment‚Äù applies only among creditors within the 
same class.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Hence, differentiation in recovery percentages between FCs and OCs, or between secured and unsecured 
creditors, does not violate equality so long as the minimum liquidation value is met for OCs.  
‚û¢‚Äã Regarding Standard Chartered Bank, the Court held that its classification as an unsecured financial creditor was 
correct.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since CoC‚Äôs distribution was based on security interests and the feasibility of the plan, SCB‚Äôs lower 
recovery did not amount to discrimination.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The CoC‚Äôs decision to approve the plan was validly taken by the requisite majority and could not be 
interfered with merely because minority creditors received lesser amounts. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On the formation of a sub-committee, the Court distinguished between delegation of core decision-making powers 
(impermissible) and delegation of administrative or negotiation-related tasks (permissible).  
‚û¢‚Äã Since the sub-committee in this case merely negotiated with ArcelorMittal and all final approvals were taken by the 
CoC itself, this arrangement was held valid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã With respect to judicial review of CoC decisions, the Court reaffirmed K. Sashidhar‚Äîthat neither NCLT nor NCLAT can 
question the merits of CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom.  
‚û¢‚Äã Their review is confined to ensuring compliance with Section 30(2)‚Äîthat the plan maximizes asset value, keeps the 
corporate debtor a going concern, and protects the interests of all stakeholders. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Finally, on the 330-day limit, the Court upheld the provision but read down ‚Äúmandatorily‚Äù to ‚Äúordinarily.‚Äù  
44 
 
‚û¢‚Äã While CIRP should normally conclude within 330 days (including litigation delays), extensions may be allowed in 
exceptional cases if delay is due to tribunal inefficiency or where resolution is nearly complete.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The mandatory reading was struck down as arbitrary under Article 14 and as an unreasonable restriction 
on the right to carry on business under Article 19(1)(g). 
 
Conclusion: SC set aside the NCLAT‚Äôs directions for equal treatment of all creditors, affirming that: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The principle of equality for all does not apply to unequals under the IBC. Equitable treatment exists only within the same 
class of creditors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Financial creditors and operational creditors can be treated differently, as long as OCs receive at least liquidation value. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The commercial wisdom of the CoC is supreme and beyond judicial interference, save for limited review under Section 
30(2). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Formation of a CoC sub-committee for administrative and negotiation purposes is valid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The 330-day CIRP limit is directory, not mandatory, and may be extended in exceptional circumstances. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court upheld the CoC-approved resolution plan, restoring the primacy of financial creditors in decision-making and 
rejecting the NCLAT‚Äôs equalization of payment structures. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Thus, the Supreme Court conclusively held that the IBC does not require identical treatment for all creditors‚Äî‚Äúequality 
cannot be stretched to treating unequals equally‚Äù‚Äîand reinforced the doctrine of commercial wisdom as the cornerstone of 
insolvency resolution in India. 
 
Karad Urban v Swwapnil [2020 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã FC filed a Section 7 IBC application before the NCLT against CD, a sugar manufacturing company.  
‚û¢‚Äã The application was admitted in January 2018, and a RP was appointed.  
‚û¢‚Äã A CoC was formed and, after multiple meetings, the CoC approved a resolution plan submitted by M/s Sai Agro 
(India) Chemicals in February 2019. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Meanwhile, the promoter/director of the CD ‚Äì who had earlier unsuccessfully challenged the CIRP initiation before the 
BHC ‚Äì filed an application seeking permission to submit his own resolution plan. This was after the CoC had already 
approved the Sai Agro plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The NCLT rejected the promoter‚Äôs application and approved Sai Agro‚Äôs plan, making it the SRA. The promoter then 
appealed to the NCLAT, which set aside the NCLT‚Äôs approval, citing several concerns: 
‚û¢‚Äã The plan‚Äôs feasibility and viability had not been adequately evaluated. 
‚û¢‚Äã There was a breach of confidentiality, as the resolution plan‚Äôs offer closely matched the liquidation 
value‚Äîsuggesting leakage of information. 
‚û¢‚Äã There was a dispute regarding ownership of an ethanol plant listed as one of the Corporate Debtor‚Äôs assets 
(belonging, allegedly, to Sarvadnya Industries Pvt. Ltd.). 
‚û¢‚Äã The invitation for resolution plans was allegedly defective and contrary to IBC Regulations. 
‚û¢‚Äã The financial creditor and RP appealed to the Supreme Court. 
 
Issue: Whether the NCLAT was justified in interfering with the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom and setting aside the approved resolution 
plan on grounds of feasibility, viability, and procedural irregularities. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 30(2) IBC: NCLT must ensure that the resolution plan meets statutory requirements but cannot assess its 
commercial soundness. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 31(1) IBC: Once a resolution plan meets Section 30(2) conditions, NCLT must approve it. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 61(3) IBC: NCLAT can interfere with an approved plan only on limited grounds such as material irregularity, 
contravention of law, or non-compliance with mandatory provisions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã K. Sashidhar v. Indian Overseas Bank (2019) 12 SCC 150: 
‚ùñ‚Äã CoC‚Äôs decision regarding feasibility and viability of a plan is non-justiciable. 
45 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT/NCLAT cannot substitute their views for the CoC‚Äôs commercial decision. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial review is limited to verifying compliance with statutory requirements, not merits of business judgment. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reaffirmed K. Sashidhar, holding that CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom enjoys near-total deference.  
‚û¢‚Äã FCs are presumed to be fully informed about the debtor‚Äôs financial position and the viability of proposed plans ‚Üí 
their decisions (made after due deliberation) constitute collective business judgments that cannot be 
second-guessed by adjudicatory authorities. 
‚ùñ‚Äã All concerns raised by the promoter [ownership of the ethanol plant, valuation discrepancies, and alleged defects in 
advertisement] had been considered by the RP, CoC, and SRA before approval.  
‚û¢‚Äã The CoC‚Äôs decision was, therefore, an informed and conscious decision, and the NCLAT had no jurisdiction to 
revisit it merely on the pretext of re-evaluating feasibility and viability. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On Feasibility and Viability 
‚û¢‚Äã CoC‚Äôs deliberation and approval after considering all relevant factors, including the ethanol plant dispute, was 
sufficient.  
‚û¢‚Äã Once the RP and CoC had consciously accounted for the possibility that the ethanol plant might not be available 
and yet chose to proceed, the NCLT and NCLAT could not revisit that commercial choice. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On Alleged Breach of Confidentiality 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT had inferred leakage of liquidation value since the SRA‚Äôs offer coincidentally matched the RP‚Äôs 
determined liquidation value BUT SC dismissed this inference, calling it contrary to common sense ‚áí a resolution 
applicant who had the benefit of leaked liquidation information would offer ‚Çπ29.74 crore which is far exceeding 
the ‚Çπ13.53 crore liquidation value.  
‚û¢‚Äã The Court further noted that minor inconsistencies, such as a typographical error in the SRA‚Äôs declaration date, 
were blown out of proportion by the NCLAT. The appellate tribunal had ‚Äúmade a mountain out of a molehill. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On Ownership of Ethanol Plant 
‚û¢‚Äã The ethanol plant in question had already been attached under SARFAESI ‚áí resolution plan did not depend on 
the ethanol plant‚Äôs inclusion and that the SRA had undertaken to continue operations regardless ‚áí thus, the 
CoC‚Äôs approval was based on sound commercial reasoning. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On Defective Advertisement ‚Üí the promoter argued that the RP‚Äôs invitation for EoI (Expression of Interest) violated 
Regulation 36A ‚Äì BUT the version of Regulation 36A applicable in March 2018 did not require publication in newspapers; 
the requirement arose only from July 2018, via amendment ‚áí hence, the promoter could not rely on a later amendment to 
invalidate an earlier process. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Supreme Court allowed the appeal and restored the NCLT‚Äôs approval of the Sai Agro resolution plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Commercial Wisdom is Supreme ‚Üí If the CoC, RP, and SRA have considered all material facts and consciously approved a 
plan, NCLT/NCLAT cannot intervene. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Feasibility and Viability are matters of business judgment, not judicial assessment. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Alleged procedural irregularities (like confidentiality breach or minor errors) are irrelevant if they do not materially affect 
the process or outcome. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Advertisement compliance must be judged by the regulatory framework in force at the relevant time, not retrospectively. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial review is confined to Section 30(2) and Section 61(3); courts cannot sit in appeal over CoC‚Äôs wisdom. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the Supreme Court reaffirmed that once a resolution plan has been approved by an informed CoC, the 
adjudicating and appellate authorities must defer to that decision, except where statutory violations are evident. 
 
Arun Kumar Jagatramka v Jindal Steel and Power Limited [2021 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã In this case, a resolution process was initiated against the CD, but the RP failed to secure an effective resolution plan, leading 
the NCLT to order liquidation ‚Üí challenged before the NCLAT and then before the Supreme Court.  
46 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Meanwhile, Mr. Arun Kumar Jagatramka, the erstwhile promoter of the CD, filed an application under Sections 230‚Äì232 
of the Companies Act, 2013, before the NCLT, proposing a scheme of compromise and arrangement between the erstwhile 
promoters and the creditors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The NCLT permitted the proposal to be considered BUT a creditor appealed to the NCLAT, contending that a promoter 
who is ineligible under Section 29A of the IBC to submit a resolution plan cannot file an application for compromise or 
arrangement under Section 230 of the Companies Act. The NCLAT accepted this contention and held that such ineligible 
promoters could not use Section 230 to regain control of the company.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Arun Kumar Jagatramka then appealed to the Supreme Court, challenging the NCLAT‚Äôs decision and arguing that Section 
230 of the Companies Act, 2013 imposes no express disqualification on any person to propose a compromise or 
arrangement ‚Üí the NCLAT had impermissibly read the ineligibility under Section 29A of the IBC into Section 230 and 
that Regulation 2B of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (Liquidation Process) Regulations, 2016 ‚Äî which bars 
ineligible persons from being party to a compromise or arrangement ‚Äî was ultra vires the parent statute. 
 
Issues:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether a person who is ineligible under Section 29A of the IBC can propose a scheme of compromise or arrangement 
under Section 230 of the Companies Act, 2013, in the course of liquidation, and  
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Regulation 2B of the 2016 Liquidation Regulations, which restricts such persons from participating in a 
compromise or arrangement, is ultra vires the Code and the Companies Act. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 29A of the IBC lays down categories of persons ineligible to be resolution applicants, primarily including promoters 
responsible for the company‚Äôs default.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 35(1)(f) of the IBC prohibits the liquidator from selling movable or immovable property or actionable claims of the 
corporate debtor to any person ineligible to be a resolution applicant.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 2B of the 2016 Liquidation Regulations allows a compromise or arrangement under Section 230 of the 
Companies Act to be proposed within 90 days from the liquidation order but includes a proviso that any person ineligible 
under the IBC to submit a resolution plan shall not be a party to such a compromise or arrangement.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 230 of the Companies Act provides for schemes of compromise or arrangement between a company and its creditors 
or members and applies even when the company is in liquidation. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã SC examined the purpose and scheme of both the IBC and the Companies Act. 
‚û¢‚Äã The disqualification under Section 29A was a legislative response to prevent errant promoters from regaining 
control of companies they had driven into insolvency.  
‚û¢‚Äã Referring to the report of the Insolvency Law Committee and earlier decisions like ArcelorMittal India Pvt. Ltd. v. 
Satish Kumar Gupta, the Court noted that Section 29A was designed as a ‚Äúsee-through provision‚Äù to look beyond 
legal formalities and identify persons in actual control who contributed to the insolvency.  
‚û¢‚Äã AND this legislative purpose did not end with the resolution process but extended to liquidation as well, as 
reflected in Section 35(1)(f), which bars the liquidator from selling the corporate debtor‚Äôs assets to ineligible 
persons. 
‚û¢‚Äã SO, the rationale behind Section 29A permeates the liquidation stage too. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regarding the interplay between Section 230 of the Companies Act and the IBC, the Court held that both must be 
harmoniously construed.  
‚û¢‚Äã While Section 230 provides a mechanism for compromise or arrangement to revive the company, when such a 
proposal arises in the context of liquidation under the IBC, it cannot be divorced from the IBC‚Äôs objectives and 
disqualification framework.  
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, Section 230 cannot be used as a means for a promoter ineligible under Section 29A to circumvent the 
Code‚Äôs prohibitions.  
47 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Rejected the analogy between compromise and withdrawal under Section 12A, clarifying that withdrawal under 
Section 12A occurs at the inception of the insolvency process and is subject to CoC approval, whereas a Section 
230 compromise arises during liquidation and serves a different statutory function. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 2B was not ultra vires because even in its absence, the same restriction would follow from a purposive reading of 
Section 29A and Section 35(1)(f).  
‚û¢‚Äã The regulation merely reflected the legislative intent to prevent backdoor entry by defaulting promoters.  
‚û¢‚Äã The assets of a company in liquidation are held custodia legis, and any compromise or arrangement must serve the 
statutory goal of legitimate revival and value maximization, not the re-entry of disqualified promoters. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Supreme Court upheld the NCLAT‚Äôs decision and dismissed the appeal.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Held that a person ineligible under Section 29A of the IBC cannot propose a scheme of compromise or arrangement under 
Section 230 of the Companies Act during liquidation.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Also upheld the validity of Regulation 2B of the 2016 Liquidation Regulations, finding it consistent with the IBC‚Äôs 
objectives.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Confirmed that the bar under Section 29A and Section 35(1)(f) extends to schemes of compromise or arrangement to 
prevent promoters responsible for corporate insolvency from regaining control through indirect means.
